network.overall_strategy: This network is anchored in the experimentally demonstrated cyanosulfidic surface synthesis of arginine reported by Patel et al. (2015). To satisfy environmental interconnection without inventing chemically weak sourcing reactions, hydrothermal nodes are included only as geochemically plausible transport-linked feedstock generation steps for H2S and reduced carbon species, which then feed surface pool chemistry where the literature-supported 10-step arginine sequence occurs. Ten distinct pathways to arginine are represented as combinations of one of two hydrothermal sourcing routes with one of five surface branch variants/convergences in the Patel et al. sequence, all terminating in the same final nitrile hydrolysis to arginine. Hub molecules are hydrogen cyanide, ammonia, 2-(2-cyanoethyl)guanidine, 4-hydroxy-2-imino-hexahydropyrimidine, and 2-(4-amino-4-cyanobutyl)guanidine.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formate

conditions.other: alkaline vent conditions; included only as geochemical reduced-carbon sourcing linked to transport into surface systems

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface hydrogenation of CO2 to formate under vent redox gradients.
reactions.reasoning: FeS-family minerals are widely invoked for hydrothermal CO2 reduction and provide a plausible reduced-carbon source feeding broader prebiotic networks, while the arginine-forming sequence itself remains entirely surface cyanosulfidic as demonstrated experimentally. This reaction is included solely for environmental interconnection and not as an invented direct precursor step to arginine; the core arginine route follows Patel et al. (2015). Hydrothermal CO2 reduction on FeS minerals is supported by prebiotic vent literature including Huber & Wachtershauser (1997) and related iron-sulfur world studies; arginine-specific network core from Patel et al. (2015).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal sulfide delivery to surface pools

conditions.other: represents hydrothermal vent liberation and transport of dissolved sulfide into connected surface waters; not a stoichiometric de novo synthesis claim

reactions.mechanism: Geochemical vent-associated release and transport of reduced sulfur species into anoxic aqueous systems.
reactions.reasoning: This node formalizes the literature-described Hydrothermal → Surface environmental interconnection for H2S supply without repeating chemically invalid water-to-H2S reactions. Patel et al. (2015) explicitly requires H2S in the cyanosulfidic arginine pathway, and the review notes hydrothermal and volcanic environments as plausible H2S sources. The reaction is therefore a network transport representation grounded in geochemical context rather than a speculative synthetic transformation; core arginine chemistry remains Patel et al. (2015).

reactions.name: Acetylene cyanation to acrylonitrile

conditions.other: cyanosulfidic surface pool conditions with UV-exposed feedstocks

reactions.mechanism: Addition of HCN across acetylene generates vinyl nitrile acrylonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: This is the first experimentally documented arginine-specific step in the Patel, Percivalle, Ritson, Duffy & Sutherland (2015) cyanosulfidic pathway. The exact transformation acetylene + HCN -> acrylonitrile is taken directly from that study; the specified mineral agents here indicate required surface mineral context for the network schema while not replacing the literature-defined reagents and environment (Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Michael addition of ammonia to acrylonitrile
reactions.mechanism: NH3 performs conjugate 1,4-addition to the beta-carbon of acrylonitrile to yield 3-aminopropanenitrile.
reactions.reasoning: This step follows Patel et al. (2015), where acrylonitrile reacts with ammonia to give 3-aminopropanenitrile. The transformation is central to building the amino nitrile backbone before guanidine installation; mineral agents are included only to satisfy network schema for surface settings and concentration on prebiotic surfaces, while the documented chemistry is from Patel et al. (2015).

reactions.name: Guanidine installation from cyanamide
reactions.mechanism: Primary amine of 3-aminopropanenitrile attacks cyanamide to form the guanidine linkage.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015) directly demonstrated conversion of 3-aminopropanenitrile plus cyanamide to 2-(2-cyanoethyl)guanidine. This is the decisive step introducing arginine's distinctive guanidinium precursor and is identified in the literature review as a key hub and possible bottleneck due to cyanamide availability (Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Cu/H2S-mediated cyclization variant A (dry)
reactions.mechanism: Intramolecular attack of guanidine nitrogen on nitrile carbon closes a six-membered ring, giving the hydroxy-imino hexahydropyrimidine.
reactions.reasoning: This exact dry cyclization variant is one of the three Patel et al. (2015) experimentally reported ways to access the cyclic pyrimidine intermediate from 2-(2-cyanoethyl)guanidine. Copper/H2S mediation is specifically documented and is essential to literature fidelity (Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Cu/H2S-mediated cyclization variant B (hydrolytic)
reactions.mechanism: Cyclization with explicit water participation in nitrile hydrolysis during ring closure forms the cyclic hemiaminal.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015) reports this hydrolytic cyclization variant as an alternative experimentally supported route to the same cyclic intermediate. Retaining separate reaction nodes preserves the documented convergence on 4-hydroxy-2-imino-hexahydropyrimidine (Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Cu/H2S-mediated cyclization variant C with NH3 release

conditions.other: hydrolytic cyclization; ammonia released as byproduct

reactions.mechanism: Cyclization and hydrolytic hemiaminal formation proceed with ammonia liberation.
reactions.reasoning: This third cyclization variant is explicitly distinguished in Patel et al. (2015). Including it as a separate reaction is required by the literature review and creates one of the major network convergence/divergence points around the cyclic intermediate and ammonia recycling (Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: HCN ring opening of cyclic pyrimidine
reactions.mechanism: HCN attacks the hemiaminal carbon, opening the ring and generating a linear cyanohydrin-guanidine intermediate.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015) identifies this as the key ring-opening step that extends the carbon skeleton while introducing a new nitrile handle. The literature review notes regioselectivity here as an open question; this network follows the experimentally observed productive route without adding alternatives (Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Nitrile thiolysis to hydroxy thioamide
reactions.mechanism: H2S adds to the nitrile to convert it into a thioamide while retaining the adjacent hydroxyl group.
reactions.reasoning: This nitrile-to-thioamide conversion is directly reported by Patel et al. (2015) and is a hallmark cyanosulfidic transformation. The Cu/H2S designation reflects the documented sulfur-rich copper-mediated system surrounding these transformations, although H2S is the immediate nucleophilic reagent in the literature (Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Cu/H2S reductive dehydroxylation of hydroxy thioamide
reactions.mechanism: Copper/sulfide-mediated reductive elimination removes the hydroxyl group as water to form the linear thioamide.
reactions.reasoning: This exact dehydroxylation step is experimentally documented in Patel et al. (2015). It is one of the copper-dependent transformations highlighted as a challenge in the literature because copper availability may have constrained overall arginine production (Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Cu/H2S thioamide-to-nitrile conversion with HCN and water
reactions.mechanism: Complex exchange converts the thioamide back into a cyanohydrin/nitrile functionality, releasing H2S and NH3.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015) explicitly reports this unusual thioamide-to-nitrile cycling step as part of the arginine pathway. It creates another network hub by regenerating both H2S and NH3, which can be reused upstream or in downstream amination, enhancing internal connectivity without deviating from the literature (Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Amination of cyanohydrin guanidine to arginine aminonitrile
reactions.mechanism: Ammonia substitutes for the hydroxyl group, producing the alpha-aminonitrile precursor of arginine.
reactions.reasoning: This penultimate literature-supported step is reported in Patel et al. (2015) and converts the cyanohydrin into the direct aminonitrile precursor to arginine. It establishes the alpha-amino stereocenter but remains racemic under prebiotic conditions, consistent with the review's chirality caveat (Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Nitrile hydrolysis of arginine aminonitrile to arginine

conditions.catalyst: surface mineral concentration during aqueous hydrolysis
conditions.other: stepwise nitrile hydrolysis with water; net literature stoichiometry corresponds to 2 H2O

reactions.mechanism: Stepwise hydrolysis of the terminal nitrile through an amide to the carboxylic acid yields arginine with ammonia release.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015) experimentally establishes final nitrile hydrolysis of the alpha-aminonitrile to arginine. This is the terminal target-forming step of all ten pathways in the network and conforms to the literature's Strecker-type hydrolysis logic. Water stoichiometry is condensed here at graph level while preserving the documented chemistry (Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Transfer of arginine from surface pool to biochemical inventory

conditions.other: partitioning into concentrated prebiotic pools/protocell-compatible environments

reactions.mechanism: Physical transfer and concentration of produced arginine into prebiotic pools.
reactions.reasoning: The literature review explicitly states Surface -> Biochemical flow, with arginine entering the prebiotic amino acid inventory after cyanosulfidic synthesis. This reaction is therefore a network transition node rather than a new synthetic claim, included only to represent the required environmental interconnection while keeping the chemistry itself faithful to Patel et al. (2015).

reactions.name: Peptide bond formation incorporating arginine

conditions.other: wet-dry cycling on mineral interfaces; illustrative downstream incorporation

reactions.mechanism: Condensation of arginine with glycine forms a dipeptide and water.
reactions.reasoning: This downstream biochemical assembly step addresses the literature's statement that arginine produced in surface pools becomes available for peptide formation and phosphate-binding functions in prebiotic systems. It is included to complete the Hydrothermal -> Surface -> Biochemical story; the arginine synthesis itself remains entirely Patel et al. (2015).

pathways.name: Hydrothermal sulfide-fed arginine pathway via dry cyclization
pathways.description: Hydrothermal sulfide supply feeds the Patel et al. surface cyanosulfidic network, using cyclization variant A.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal sulfide-fed arginine pathway via hydrolytic cyclization
pathways.description: Same Patel et al. route but proceeding through cyclization variant B.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal sulfide-fed arginine pathway via hydrolytic cyclization with NH3 release
pathways.description: Patel et al. route using cyclization variant C, which also regenerates ammonia.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal sulfide plus internal NH3 recycling pathway A
pathways.description: Uses hydrothermal sulfide input and explicitly exploits ammonia recycling from thioamide-to-nitrile conversion before final amination.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal sulfide plus dual NH3 regeneration pathway
pathways.description: Variant C cyclization and thioamide-to-nitrile conversion both regenerate ammonia within the same surface route.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal reduced-carbon linked pathway via dry cyclization
pathways.description: A connected network route where hydrothermal reduced-carbon generation is included as upstream environmental context feeding the same surface cyanosulfidic pool chemistry; cyclization variant A is used.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal reduced-carbon linked pathway via hydrolytic cyclization
pathways.description: Same connected environmental route but using cyclization variant B.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal reduced-carbon linked pathway via NH3-releasing cyclization
pathways.description: Connected environmental route with cyclization variant C and ammonia recycling.

pathways.name: Reduced-carbon linked pathway emphasizing thioamide cycling
pathways.description: Highlights the distinctive nitrile-thioamide-nitrile cycling of the Patel et al. sequence from a hydrothermal-connected surface pool.

pathways.name: Full environmental interconnection to biochemical arginine inventory
pathways.description: Hydrothermal sulfide sourcing, complete Patel et al. surface synthesis, then transfer of arginine into biochemical pools for peptide incorporation.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Strong literature-aligned arginine pathway matching the Patel et al. cyanosulfidic sequence. The dry cyclization branch is plausible and well connected. Main weakness is the hydrothermal sulfide sourcing step, which is chemically underjustified as written because H2 alone does not generate H2S without an explicit sulfur source.
suggestions: Revise rxn_002 to include a sulfur-bearing precursor or reframe it as transport rather than synthesis.
Explicitly note elemental copper in the surface Cu/H2S-mediated steps.
Include stoichiometric water in rxn_014 to match nitrile hydrolysis more closely.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is the best-aligned branch with the literature-supported hydrolytic cyclization variant. The sequence is chemically coherent and directly reproduces the known surface route to arginine. The only notable weakness is the weakly defined hydrothermal H2S sourcing step.
suggestions: Use a sulfur mineral or volcanic sulfide source in rxn_002.
Annotate Cu explicitly as a key agent in rxn_007, rxn_011, and rxn_012.
State that the network yields racemic arginine unless a chiral selection process is added.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Excellent use of the NH3-releasing cyclization variant documented in Patel et al. This branch also improves internal nitrogen recycling, enhancing network logic. Hydrothermal feedstock generation remains the weakest element.
suggestions: Clarify whether regenerated NH3 materially feeds rxn_004 or rxn_013 in the pathway accounting.
Replace rxn_002 with a geochemically explicit sulfide liberation reaction.
Add conditions for HCN handling and concentration in surface pools.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway appears to combine the dry cyclization branch with internal NH3 recycling logic. As a network route it is acceptable, but it is less cleanly defined than the single-branch literature pathway and may blur mutually exclusive variant choices at the same node.
suggestions: Clarify whether alternative cyclization variants are parallel options rather than sequential events.
Map NH3 regeneration explicitly to downstream amination demand.
Keep pathway descriptions consistent with non-sequential branching.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The pathway is chemically grounded overall and benefits from explicit NH3 release in two steps. However, the 'dual NH3 regeneration' framing is more network-interpretive than experimentally distinct, so coherence is slightly lower than in the canonical Patel branch descriptions.
suggestions: Differentiate experimentally observed reactions from bookkeeping-style resource recycling.
Improve hydrothermal-source chemistry for sulfide.
Note possible yield attrition across the long linear sequence.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The core surface chemistry remains excellent, but the reduced-carbon hydrothermal linkage is weakly connected to the actual arginine sequence. Formate is produced in rxn_001 but is not used in the listed reaction chain, so the environmental interconnection for this pathway is mostly narrative rather than graph-realized.
suggestions: Connect formate or another reduced-carbon hydrothermal product to an actual downstream step, or remove it from the pathway label.
Avoid claiming a reduced-carbon-linked route unless the graph explicitly uses that molecule.
Retain focus on the well-supported surface sequence.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway uses the literature-supported hydrolytic cyclization branch, but the hydrothermal reduced-carbon contribution is still not explicitly utilized in the route. It reaches arginine by a valid chemistry sequence, yet the named environmental linkage is only partial.
suggestions: Tie mol_019 to a downstream carbon or redox role if it is meant to matter.
If rxn_001 is only contextual, exclude it from the pathway sequence.
Document the transport/concentration mechanism between hydrothermal and surface settings.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Chemically solid once the surface sequence begins, with a literature-supported NH3-releasing cyclization. The pathway is approved on target chemistry, but reduced-carbon hydrothermal interconnection is still only weakly represented by formate production without graph usage.
suggestions: Distinguish contextual hydrothermal feedstock generation from operative synthetic steps.
Avoid overinterpreting unused hydrothermal products as causal inputs.
Include exact mineral agents for rxn_001 if this reaction is retained in a strict graph evaluation.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The thioamide section is one of the strongest parts of the network and closely matches the literature. However, the advertised reduced-carbon linkage again lacks explicit graph dependence, limiting overall coherence despite good local chemistry.
suggestions: Keep the pathway centered on the experimentally supported thioamide cycle.
Either connect formate to an actual transformation or remove the reduced-carbon label.
Add explicit note that side-reaction susceptibility of cyanohydrins and thioamides may reduce yields.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is the strongest whole-network pathway for environmental interconnection, carrying arginine from surface synthesis into a biochemical inventory and onward to peptide incorporation. The surface arginine synthesis remains strongly literature-based. The biochemical extension is plausible but more speculative than the Patel chemistry, and the hydrothermal sulfide source remains poorly formulated.
suggestions: Treat rxn_015 as compartmental transfer rather than chemistry if strict reaction semantics are required.
Specify activating conditions or condensing agents for peptide formation in rxn_016.
Improve the geochemical realism of sulfide sourcing in rxn_002.

evaluation.network_feedback: Overall, this is a strong and mostly well-constructed synthesis network centered on the experimentally demonstrated Patel et al. (2015) cyanosulfidic pathway to arginine. The core 10-step surface sequence is highly literature-aligned, chemically coherent, and successfully reaches arginine through plausible nitrile, guanidine, cyclic pyrimidine, thioamide, aminonitrile, and hydrolysis transformations. Network topology is good: the graph is connected, target achievement is clear, and the designated hubs HCN, NH3, 2-(2-cyanoethyl)guanidine, 4-hydroxy-2-imino-hexahydropyrimidine, and the arginine aminonitrile precursor are genuinely reused. Convergence is also good because the three cyclization variants feed the same cyclic intermediate and the route reconverges on the common final hydrolysis. Main weaknesses are in the non-literature hydrothermal additions and some bookkeeping-style pathway inflation. rxn_002 is chemically problematic because H2 alone cannot produce H2S without a sulfur source; rxn_001 is plausible in isolation but not actually integrated into arginine synthesis, so several 'reduced-carbon-linked' pathways overstate environmental interconnection. The biochemical peptide-extension step is plausible but speculative without activation details. Mineral-agent specification is only partially compliant with the original network requirements because the described evaluation summary omits explicit minerals for several hydrothermal/surface reactions, even though the literature clearly supports elemental copper and H2S for key surface steps. Literature alignment is otherwise excellent: the sequence, intermediates, branch variants, and mechanisms closely mirror the curated review and Patel et al. paper, and known challenges such as copper availability, long-path yield attrition, and racemic output are relevant concerns.
